text: This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Phil.
zh: 我是菲尔。

text: And I'm Becca.
zh: 我是贝卡。

text: 6 Minute English listeners are truly global, coming from all over the world, including some of the coldest countries on Earth – places like Finland, where winter temperatures drop to minus 20 degrees Celsius.
zh: 《六分钟英语》的听众真的是遍布全球,来自世界各地,包括地球上一些最寒冷的国家,比如芬兰,那里冬天气温会降到零下20摄氏度。

text: Are you good at dealing with the cold, Phil?
zh: 菲尔,你擅长应对寒冷吗?

text: I'm not sure, but I do know that I don't like it.
zh: 我不太确定,但我知道我不喜欢冷。

text: What about you, Becca?
zh: 贝卡,你呢?

text: Well, I'd rather be too hot and cool down than too cold and try to warm up.
zh: 嗯,我宁愿先热一点再让自己凉下来,也不想先冷再想办法暖和起来。

text: Yes, me too.
zh: 是的,我也是。

text: Here in the UK, it never gets as cold as Finland, but it's not unusual to see some people dressed in t -shirts while others are wrapped up in warm clothes.
zh: 在英国,天气从来没有芬兰那么冷,但看到有人穿着T恤,而另一些人却裹着厚厚的衣服,这并不稀奇。

text: Why do people feel the cold so differently?
zh: 为什么人们对寒冷的感受如此不同呢?

text: That's what we'll be discussing in in this episode, as well as learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: 这就是我们这期节目要讨论的内容,同时我们也会学习一些实用的新词汇和短语。

text: And, as always, you'll find all the vocabulary from this episode, plus a quiz and worksheet, on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 一如既往,你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期节目的所有词汇,还有测验和练习题。

text: But now I have a question for you, Becca.
zh: 不过现在,贝卡,我有个问题要问你。

text: According to the Guinness Book of World Records, the lowest temperature ever recorded was a very cold minus 89 degrees Celsius.
zh: 根据《吉尼斯世界纪录》,地球上有记录以来的最低气温是零下89摄氏度,非常寒冷。

text: But where?
zh: 但那是在哪里呢?

text: Was it A. The Arctic, B. Antarctica, or C. The top of Mount Everest?
zh: 是A. 北极,B. 南极洲,还是C. 珠穆朗玛峰峰顶?

text: Mmm, I'm going to guess B. Antarctica.
zh: 嗯,我猜是B,南极洲。

text: OK, well we'll find out the answer at the end of the programme.
zh: 好的,我们会在节目最后揭晓答案。

text: Our experience of cold depends on many things, including our genes, culture and place of birth.
zh: 我们对寒冷的感受取决于很多因素,包括基因、文化和出生地。

text: But perhaps the most obvious thing is the clothes we wear.
zh: 但也许最明显的因素是我们穿的衣服。

text: Professor Gnid Sartren lives on the icy island of Svalbard, 800 miles inside the Arctic Circle.
zh: Gnid Sartren教授住在斯瓦尔巴群岛这个冰天雪地的岛屿上,那里在北极圈以内800英里处。

text: Here she advises presenter Caroline Steele on how to dress against the cold for BBC World Service programme CrowdScience.
zh: 在这里,她为BBC世界服务台的节目《CrowdScience》主持人卡罗琳·斯蒂尔提供了如何穿衣御寒的建议。

text: So what should we consider when choosing our clothes?
zh: 那么我们在选择衣服时应该考虑什么呢?

text: First and foremost, I would say that perhaps the gender differs.
zh: 我想说,首先,也是最重要的一点,可能是性别的差异。

text: So, male often are more tolerant perhaps than females.
zh: 所以,男性往往比女性更耐寒一些。

text: Then it's of course what you're used to.
zh: 然后当然还有你习不习惯的问题。

text: Are you used to dealing with the cold?
zh: 你是否习惯应对寒冷?

text: Are you not?
zh: 还是不习惯呢?

text: Gunhild says that first and foremost, men and women filled the cold differently.
zh: 冈希尔德说,首先且最重要的是,男性和女性对寒冷的感受不同。

text: She uses the phrase first and foremost to mean more than anything else.
zh: 她用“first and foremost”这个短语来表示“比其他任何事都更重要”。

text: She wants to emphasise that something, in this case someone's gender, is the most important thing to consider.
zh: 她想强调的是,在这种情况下,一个人的性别是需要考虑的最重要因素。

text: There is some debate about whether men or women are more tolerant of the cold.
zh: 关于男性和女性谁更耐寒,存在一些争论。

text: Being tolerant of something means being able to endure it without getting hurt.
zh: 对某事有耐受力,意味着能够承受它而不受伤害。

text: Another important factor is whether you are used to the cold, if being in cold environments is something you're familiar with.
zh: 另一个重要因素是你是否已经习惯了寒冷,也就是身处寒冷环境对你来说是否是件熟悉的事。

text: It might sound obvious that someone born in Arctic Svalbard would feel less cold than someone born in Brazil.
zh: 一个在北极斯瓦尔巴出生的人,比一个在巴西出生的人更不怕冷,这听起来似乎理所当然。

text: ill.
zh: (此处为无意义片段。)

text: But apart from environmental factors, are there actual physical differences that allow people to cope better with the cold?
zh: 但除了环境因素之外,是否真的存在生理上的差异,使人们能更好地应对寒冷呢?

text: Dr. Kara Okobok studies reindeer herders in northern Finland, people who live in cold temperatures every day and have done for centuries.
zh: 卡拉·奥科博克博士研究芬兰北部的驯鹿牧民,这些人几个世纪以来每天都生活在寒冷的气温中。

text: She measures their reaction to extreme cold and compares it to ordinary Finns from warmer parts of the country.
zh: 她测量了他们对极端寒冷的反应,并将其与来自该国较温暖地区的普通芬兰人进行比较。

text: Here, Kara shares her findings with Caroline Still from BBC World Service's CrowdScience.
zh: 在这里,卡拉向BBC世界服务台《CrowdScience》节目的卡罗琳·斯蒂尔分享了她的研究发现。

text: Okay, and my guess is the reindeer herders deal better in the cold?
zh: 好的,我猜驯鹿牧民更能应对寒冷?

text: Yes and no!
zh: 是,也不完全是!

text: The more data we collect within this area, the more confusing the picture gets.
zh: 我们在这个领域收集的数据越多,情况反而变得越令人困惑。

text: I can say that subjectively, at this point, the reindeer herders at least kind of mentally handle the cold far better.
zh: 我可以说,从主观感受上看,目前驯鹿牧民至少在心理上更能应对寒冷。

text: They are far less likely to shiver.
zh: 他们打寒颤的可能性要小得多。

text: So, do the reindeer herders deal better with the cold?
zh: 那么,驯鹿牧民真的更能应对寒冷吗?

text: The answer is yes and no.
zh: 答案是:是,也不完全是。

text: A phrase meaning partly and partly not, used when there's no clear answer to a question.
zh: 这个短语的意思是“部分是,部分不是”,用于当一个问题没有明确答案的时候。

text: However, Kara does say, subjectively, the herders manage better.
zh: 不过,卡拉确实说,从主观上讲,牧民们应对得更好。

text: They don't feel so cold.
zh: 他们并没有感觉那么冷。

text: Subjectively means based on your personal inner experience, rather than objective facts.
zh: “subjectively”(主观地)意味着基于个人的内心感受,而不是客观事实。

text: And physically, there are differences too.
zh: 而在生理上,也确实存在差异。

text: Reindeer herders are less likely to shiver – the shaky movement that cold muscles make to try and warm them up.
zh: 驯鹿牧民打寒颤的可能性更小——寒颤是寒冷的肌肉为了让自己暖和起来而产生的颤抖动作。

text: In fact, how we experience the cold is probably a combination of everything we've discussed, including genetic adaptations passed on from parents to children.
zh: 事实上,我们体验寒冷的方式,可能是我们讨论过的所有因素的综合结果,包括从父母遗传给孩子的基因适应性。

text: Right, I'm off to find my gloves and woolly hat, so why don't you reveal the answer to the question, Phil?
zh: 好了,我要去找我的手套和毛线帽了,菲尔,不如你来揭晓那个问题的答案吧?

text: Yes, I asked where the lowest temperature on Earth was ever recorded.
zh: 好的,我之前问的是,地球上有记录以来的最低气温是在哪里测得的。

text: You said B, Antarctica.
zh: 你说的是B,南极洲。

text: And that is the right answer.
zh: 这就是正确答案。

text: The lowest temperature ever recorded on Earth was at the Vostok Research Station in Antarctica in 1983.
zh: 地球上有记录以来的最低气温,是1983年在南极洲的沃斯托克科考站测得的。

text: Let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt, starting with the phrase first and foremost, meaning more than anything else.
zh: 让我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇,首先是短语first and foremost,意思是“比其他任何事都更重要”。

text: A person who is tolerant of something is able to endure it without being hurt.
zh: 对某事tolerant(耐受)的人,能够承受它而不受伤害。

text: If you're used to something, you're familiar with it.
zh: 如果你used to(习惯)某事,就说明你对它很熟悉。

text: The idiom yes and no means partly and partly not, and is used when you can't give a clear answer to a question.
zh: 习语yes and no意思是“部分是,部分不是”,用于当你无法对一个问题给出明确答案时。

text: The adverb subjectively means in a way that's based on your personal experience, rather than objective facts.
zh: 副词subjectively意思是“基于个人体验”,而不是基于客观事实。

text: And finally, a shiver is the shaking movement made by your muscles when you you feel cold or afraid.
zh: 最后,shiver(寒颤)是指当你感到寒冷或害怕时,肌肉产生的颤抖动作。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up.
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了。

text: But remember, you'll find a quiz and a worksheet for this episode on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 不过别忘了,你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期节目的测验和练习题。

text: See you there soon, but for now, it's goodbye.
zh: 我们网站上很快见,不过现在,先说再见。

text: Bye!
zh: 拜拜!

text: Six Minute English, from bbclearningenglish .com
zh: 《六分钟英语》,来自bbclearningenglish.com
